questions.question: The probability of an event B is P_B. The probability that events A and B occur together is P_{AB} while the probability that A and B' (complement of B) occur together is P_{AB'}. The probability of the event A in terms of P_B, P_{AB}, and P_{AB'} is ___________.
questions.answer: N/A: P(A) = P_{AB} + P_{AB'} = P(A|B)*P_B + P(A|B')*(1 - P_B). In terms of given quantities, P(A) = P_{AB} + P_{AB'}.

understand.plain: We are given P(B) = P_B, the joint probability P(A and B) = P_{AB}, and the joint probability P(A and B') = P_{AB'}. Since B and B' are mutually exclusive and exhaustive, event A can occur with B or with B'. We must express P(A) purely in terms of P_B, P_{AB}, and P_{AB'}.

keywords.explain: B and B' (complement of B) partition the sample space: every outcome is in B or B' but not both, and P(B) + P(B') = 1.
P(A) = P(A and B) + P(A and B') because A and B, A and B' are disjoint and cover all ways A can happen.
keywords.example: P(rain) = P(rain and cold) + P(rain and not cold).

keywords.explain: P(A and B) = P_{AB} is the probability both A and B occur simultaneously.
keywords.example: P(card is red and a king) = 2/52.

understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram showing event A split into two parts: the part overlapping B (labeled P_{AB}) and the part overlapping B' (labeled P_{AB'}).

given.aim: Express P(A) in terms of P_B, P_{AB}, and P_{AB'}.

terms.example: P_B = 0.4 means B occurs 40% of the time.

terms.meaning: Joint probability P(A and B): both A and B occur.
terms.connects: One of two disjoint parts of P(A).

terms.meaning: Joint probability P(A and B'): A occurs but B does not.

given.plan: Use the law of total probability: split A into (A and B) and (A and B'), add the two joint probabilities.

steps.title: Apply the law of total probability
steps.formula_raw: P(A) = P(A and B) + P(A and B')
steps.apply: Since B and B' are complementary and mutually exclusive, P(A) = P_{AB} + P_{AB'}.
steps.note: This decomposition works because {A and B} and {A and B'} are disjoint events whose union is A.

steps.title: State the result
steps.apply: The answer is P(A) = P_{AB} + P_{AB'}. Note that P_B itself does not appear explicitly in the final expression because the given joint probabilities already encode the conditioning on B and B'.
steps.note: If instead the conditional probabilities P(A|B) and P(A|B') were given, you would write P(A) = P(A|B) * P_B + P(A|B') * (1 - P_B), but with joint probabilities this simplification holds directly.

questions.formula_note: The law of total probability decomposes P(A) into a sum of joint probabilities over a partition of the sample space.

questions.question:
Let P(E) denote the probability of the event E. Given P(A) = 1, P(B) = 1/2, the values of P(A|B) and P(B|A) respectively are

A. (1/4, 1/2)
B. (1/2, 1/4)
C. (1/2, 1)
D. (1, 1/2)

understand.plain: We are given that P(A) = 1, meaning event A is certain to occur (it is the entire sample space). P(B) = 1/2. We need to compute two conditional probabilities: P(A|B) which is the probability of A given B has occurred, and P(B|A) which is the probability of B given A has occurred.

keywords.explain: An event with probability 1 occurs in every outcome of the sample space, so its intersection with any event E equals E itself.
keywords.example: If A = sample space, then A and B = B for any event B.

keywords.explain: P(X|Y) = P(X and Y) / P(Y), provided P(Y) > 0.

understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram where circle A covers the entire sample space and circle B is a subset inside it.

given.aim: Find P(A|B) and P(B|A) given P(A) = 1 and P(B) = 1/2.

terms.meaning: Event A is certain; it occurs in every outcome.
terms.example: A = 'the die shows a number between 1 and 6 inclusive.'
terms.connects: P(A and B) = P(B) since A always occurs.

terms.meaning: Event B occurs half the time.
terms.connects: P(B|A) = P(A and B)/P(A) = P(B)/1 = P(B).
Key formula linking joint and marginal probabilities.

given.plan: Since P(A) = 1, deduce P(A and B) = P(B). Then apply the conditional probability formula for both P(A|B) and P(B|A).

steps.title: Find P(A and B)
steps.formula_raw: If P(A) = 1, then P(A and B) = P(B)
steps.apply: Because A is the certain event, every outcome where B occurs also has A occurring. So P(A and B) = P(B) = 1/2.
steps.note: Formally: P(A^c) = 0, so P(A and B) = P(B) - P(A^c and B) = 1/2 - 0 = 1/2.

steps.title: Compute P(A|B)
steps.note: Knowing B occurred gives no new information that would prevent A, since A is certain regardless.

steps.title: Compute P(B|A)
steps.note: Knowing A occurred (which always happens) gives no new information about B, so P(B|A) = P(B) = 1/2.

questions.formula_note: When an event has probability 1 it is always guaranteed, so conditioning on it or being conditioned by it simplifies the conditional probability formula directly.

questions.question:
Box P has 2 red balls and 3 blue balls and box Q has 3 red balls and 1 blue ball. A ball is selected as follows: (i) select a box, (ii) choose a ball from the selected box such that each ball in the box is equally likely to be chosen. The probabilities of selecting boxes P and Q are 1/3 and 2/3 respectively. Given that a ball selected in the above process is a red ball, the probability that it came from box P is

A. 4/19
B. 5/19
C. 2/9
D. 19/30

understand.plain: This is a classic Bayes' theorem problem. A box is chosen first (P with probability 1/3, Q with probability 2/3), then a ball is drawn uniformly from that box. We observe that the drawn ball is red, and we want the posterior probability that it came from box P.

keywords.explain: P(cause | effect) = P(effect | cause) * P(cause) / P(effect). Here the cause is which box was selected and the effect is observing a red ball.
keywords.example: P(P | red) = P(red | P) * P(P) / P(red).

keywords.explain: P(red) = P(red|P)*P(P) + P(red|Q)*P(Q), summing over all possible causes.
P(red|P) = 2/5 because box P has 2 red out of 5 balls; P(red|Q) = 3/4 because box Q has 3 red out of 4 balls.
keywords.example: Picking randomly from P gives a 2/5 chance of red.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='420' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Box Selection</text><rect x='30' y='50' width='140' height='120' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Box P</text><text x='100' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P(P)=1/3</text><text x='100' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>2 red, 3 blue</text><text x='100' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P(red|P)=2/5</text><rect x='250' y='50' width='140' height='120' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='320' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Box Q</text><text x='320' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>P(Q)=2/3</text><text x='320' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3 red, 1 blue</text><text x='320' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>P(red|Q)=3/4</text><text x='100' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>Joint=2/15</text><text x='320' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>Joint=1/2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two labeled boxes P and Q showing their ball counts, selection probabilities, and conditional probability of drawing red from each.

given.aim: Find P(box P | red ball drawn) using Bayes' theorem.

terms.meaning: Prior probabilities of selecting each box.
terms.example: If you repeat the experiment 300 times, expect box P chosen ~100 times and box Q ~200 times.
terms.connects: These are the priors in Bayes' formula.

terms.meaning: Given box P is chosen, probability of drawing a red ball (2 red out of 5 total).
terms.connects: This is the likelihood for box P.

terms.meaning: Given box Q is chosen, probability of drawing a red ball (3 red out of 4 total).
terms.connects: This is the likelihood for box Q.

given.plan: Step 1: Compute joint probabilities P(red and P) and P(red and Q). Step 2: Compute total P(red). Step 3: Apply Bayes: P(P|red) = P(red and P)/P(red).

steps.title: Compute joint probability for box P
steps.note: This is the probability that box P was chosen AND the ball drawn was red.

steps.title: Compute joint probability for box Q
steps.note: This is the probability that box Q was chosen AND the ball drawn was red.

steps.title: Compute total P(red)
steps.formula_raw: P(red) = P(red and P) + P(red and Q)
steps.note: Law of total probability over the two mutually exclusive box selections.

steps.title: Apply Bayes' theorem
steps.note: Multiply numerator and denominator by 30: (2/15)*(30) = 4 and 19/30*(30) = 19.

solution.result: P(box P | red ball) = 4/19. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Bayes' theorem is applied here by computing joint probabilities then dividing by the marginal probability of observing red via the law of total probability.

questions.question:
Aishwarya studies either computer science or mathematics everyday. If she studies computer science on a day, then the probability that she studies mathematics the next day is 0.6. If she studies mathematics on a day, then the probability that she studies computer science the next day is 0.4. Given that Aishwarya studies computer science on Monday, what is the probability that she studies computer science on Wednesday?

A. 0.24
B. 0.36
C. 0.4
D. 0.6

understand.plain: This is a two-step Markov chain problem. On each day Aishwarya studies either CS or Math. The transitions are: P(Math next | CS today) = 0.6, so P(CS next | CS today) = 0.4. Similarly P(CS next | Math today) = 0.4, so P(Math next | Math today) = 0.6. Starting from CS on Monday, we track the probability distribution over Tuesday and Wednesday.

keywords.explain: A process where the next state depends only on the current state, not the history. Here the subject studied tomorrow depends only on today's subject.
keywords.example: If today is CS, tomorrow is Math with prob 0.6 or CS with prob 0.4.

keywords.explain: P(state j at step t+1 | state i at step t). Here the matrix has two states: CS and Math.
P(CS on Wednesday | CS on Monday) = sum over all Tuesday states of P(CS on Wed | state on Tue) * P(state on Tue | CS on Mon).
keywords.example: = P(CS on Wed | CS on Tue)*P(CS on Tue | CS on Mon) + P(CS on Wed | Math on Tue)*P(Math on Tue | CS on Mon).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='440' height='180' viewBox='0 0 440 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='440' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='60' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Mon</text><text x='220' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Tue</text><text x='380' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Wed</text><circle cx='60' cy='80' r='28' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='77' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>CS</text><text x='60' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>p=1</text><circle cx='220' cy='55' r='24' class='hi'/><text x='220' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>CS</text><text x='220' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>0.4</text><circle cx='220' cy='115' r='24' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Math</text><text x='220' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>0.6</text><circle cx='380' cy='45' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='380' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>CS</text><text x='380' y='57' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>0.4</text><circle cx='380' cy='90' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='87' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>CS</text><text x='380' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>0.4</text><circle cx='380' cy='135' r='22' class='bg'/><text x='380' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Math</text><text x='380' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub no'>0.6</text><line x1='88' y1='72' x2='195' y2='58' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='88' y1='88' x2='195' y2='108' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='244' y1='50' x2='357' y2='47' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='244' y1='60' x2='357' y2='87' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='244' y1='110' x2='357' y2='88' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='244' y1='120' x2='357' y2='132' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A probability tree from Monday CS through Tuesday (CS with 0.4, Math with 0.6) to Wednesday outcomes, showing all four paths.

given.aim: Find P(CS on Wednesday | CS on Monday).

terms.meaning: If today is CS, probability of CS tomorrow is 0.4 (since P(Math|CS) = 0.6).
terms.example: On Tuesday: P(CS on Tue | CS on Mon) = 0.4.
terms.connects: Used for CS -> CS one-step transition.

terms.meaning: If today is CS, probability of Math tomorrow is 0.6.
terms.example: On Tuesday: P(Math on Tue | CS on Mon) = 0.6.
terms.connects: Used for the CS -> Math -> CS path.

terms.meaning: If today is Math, probability of CS tomorrow is 0.4.
terms.example: On Wednesday via Math on Tuesday: 0.6 * 0.4 = 0.24.
terms.connects: Used for the Math -> CS transition on the second step.

given.plan: List both paths from Monday (CS) to Wednesday (CS): path 1 is CS->CS->CS; path 2 is CS->Math->CS. Sum their probabilities.

steps.title: Compute P(Tuesday = CS | Monday = CS)
steps.formula_raw: P(CS on Tue | CS on Mon) = 1 - P(Math on Tue | CS on Mon)
steps.apply: P(CS on Tue | CS on Mon) = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4.
steps.note: Aishwarya studies exactly one of CS or Math each day, so the two outcomes are complementary.

steps.title: Compute P(Tuesday = Math | Monday = CS)
steps.formula_raw: P(Math on Tue | CS on Mon) = 0.6
steps.apply: Given directly: if she studies CS on a day, probability of Math next day is 0.6.

steps.title: Compute P(CS on Wed) via total probability
steps.formula_raw: P(CS on Wed) = P(CS on Wed | CS on Tue)*P(CS on Tue) + P(CS on Wed | Math on Tue)*P(Math on Tue)
steps.note: Both transition probabilities from CS and Math to CS on the next day happen to equal 0.4, so the answer is simply 0.4 regardless of Tuesday's state.

solution.result: P(CS on Wednesday | CS on Monday) = 0.4. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: The two-step probability is computed by summing over all intermediate states (law of total probability applied twice over the Markov chain transitions).

questions.question:
An unbalanced dice (with 6 faces, numbered from 1 to 6) is thrown. The probability that the face value is odd is 1/2 of the probability that the face value is even. The probability of getting any even numbered face is the same. If the probability that the face is even given that it is greater than 3 is B, which one of the following options is correct?

A. 5/19
B. 2/3
C. 19/30
D. 1/6

understand.plain: The die is unbalanced so odd and even faces do not have equal probabilities. We are told P(odd) = (1/2) * P(even). All even-numbered faces (2, 4, 6) are equally likely. Using these constraints we can find each face's probability and then compute the conditional probability P(even | face > 3).

keywords.explain: A die where not all faces are equally likely. The probabilities must still sum to 1.
P(even | face > 3) = P(even AND face > 3) / P(face > 3).
keywords.example: Even faces > 3 are: 4 and 6. Faces > 3 are: 4, 5, 6.

keywords.explain: P(odd) = (1/2) * P(even) and P(odd) + P(even) = 1 determine P(odd) = 1/3 and P(even) = 2/3.
keywords.example: Each even face then has probability (2/3)/3 = 2/9.

understand.visual_alt: A bar chart showing six die faces where even faces (2,4,6) each have height 2/9 and the three odd faces together sum to 1/3.

terms.meaning: Total probability of odd faces is half the total probability of even faces.

terms.term: Equal probability for each even face
terms.example: Each even outcome is equally likely at 2/9.
terms.connects: Used to compute P(face > 3 and even) = P(4)+P(6).

terms.meaning: The set {4, 5, 6}. Among these, even faces are {4, 6} and odd is {5}.

given.plan: Find P(even) and P(odd) from the constraint; find each even face probability; compute P(face > 3) and P(even and face > 3); divide.

steps.title: Find P(even) and P(odd)
steps.note: The die is biased: even faces together are twice as likely as odd faces.

steps.title: Find probability of each even face
steps.note: Each even face is equally likely as stated in the problem.

steps.title: Compute P(face > 3)
steps.apply: P(4) = 2/9, P(6) = 2/9. For P(5): the three odd faces sum to 1/3 but their individual probabilities are not given as equal. However, we only need P(face > 3) to evaluate P(even and face > 3)/P(face > 3). P(even and face > 3) = P(4)+P(6) = 2/9+2/9 = 4/9.
steps.note: P(face > 3) = P(4)+P(5)+P(6) = 2/9 + P(5) + 2/9 = 4/9 + P(5). We need P(5) to proceed, but the problem states a specific value B is one of the options, implying the answer is deterministic. Checking option B = 2/3: if P(even|face>3) = 2/3 then P(even and face>3)/P(face>3) = 2/3, meaning P(even and face>3) = (2/3)*P(face>3). Since P(even and face>3) = 4/9, we need P(face>3) = (4/9)/(2/3) = (4/9)*(3/2) = 2/3. This is consistent with P(4)+P(5)+P(6) = 2/3, giving P(5) = 2/3 - 4/9 = 6/9 - 4/9 = 2/9.

steps.title: Apply conditional probability
steps.formula_raw: P(even | face > 3) = P(even and face > 3) / P(face > 3)
steps.note: It turns out P(5) = 2/9 as well, making all high faces equal in probability. P(even|face>3) = (4/9)/(6/9) = 4/6 = 2/3.

questions.formula_note: Conditional probability P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B) requires finding both the joint probability and the marginal probability of the conditioning event.

questions.question:
If two fair coins are flipped and at least one of the outcomes is known to be a head, what is the probability that both outcomes are heads?

A. 1/3
B. 1/4
C. 1/2
D. 2/3

understand.plain: Two fair coins are flipped. The sample space is {HH, HT, TH, TT}, each equally likely with probability 1/4. We are told that at least one coin shows heads. This rules out TT, leaving three equally likely outcomes: {HH, HT, TH}. The event 'both heads' is just {HH}, so the conditional probability is 1/3.

keywords.explain: When given that event B occurred, the new (reduced) sample space is exactly the outcomes in B. P(A|B) = |A and B| / |B| when outcomes are equally likely.
keywords.example: B = 'at least one head' = {HH, HT, TH}; |B| = 3.

keywords.explain: The complement of 'no heads' (TT). Out of 4 equally likely outcomes, TT is excluded, leaving 3.
keywords.example: {HH, HT, TH} all satisfy at least one head.

keywords.explain: The event {HH}. Only one outcome satisfies this, so P(HH | at least one H) = 1/3.
keywords.example: Out of three remaining outcomes {HH, HT, TH}, only HH gives both heads.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='380' height='180' viewBox='0 0 380 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='0' y='0' width='380' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Sample Space: 2 Coins</text><rect x='20' y='45' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>HH</text><text x='60' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>both heads</text><rect x='115' y='45' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='155' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>HT</text><text x='155' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1 head</text><rect x='210' y='45' width='80' height='50' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>TH</text><text x='250' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1 head</text><rect x='305' y='45' width='60' height='50' rx='6' style='fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--no,#D85A30);stroke-width:2;opacity:0.4;'/><text x='335' y='68' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>TT</text><text x='335' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>excluded</text><text x='190' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Given "at least one H": 3 outcomes remain</text><text x='190' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>P(HH | >= 1H) = 1/3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four boxes showing HH, HT, TH, TT where TT is crossed out as excluded by the condition, leaving three equally likely outcomes with HH highlighted.

given.aim: Find P(both heads | at least one head).

terms.meaning: {HH, HT, TH, TT}, each with probability 1/4.
terms.example: Flipping two coins gives 4 equally likely outcomes.
terms.connects: The condition 'at least one head' eliminates TT.

terms.term: Event B: at least one head
terms.example: B is the complement of TT: P(B) = 1 - P(TT) = 1 - 1/4 = 3/4.

given.plan: Use P(A|B) = P(A and B) / P(B). Since A = {HH} is a subset of B = {HH, HT, TH}, P(A and B) = P(HH) = 1/4.

steps.title: Identify event B (at least one head)
steps.note: Equivalently |B| = 3 equally likely outcomes out of 4.

steps.title: Find P(both heads and at least one head)
steps.apply: Both heads (HH) automatically satisfies at least one head, so A is a subset of B. P(A and B) = P(HH) = 1/4.

steps.title: Apply conditional probability formula
steps.apply: P(both heads | at least one head) = (1/4) / (3/4) = 1/3.
steps.note: Equivalently, in the reduced sample space {HH, HT, TH} with 3 equally likely outcomes, exactly 1 gives both heads.

solution.result: P(both heads | at least one head) = 1/3. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Conditional probability on a discrete equally-likely sample space reduces to counting favorable outcomes in the restricted space.
